ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for steamed lobster as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared crustacean dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of steamed lobster is approximately 150 g of edible meat, equivalent to roughly half a medium-sized whole lobster.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15147)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for lobster cooked by moist heat (steaming/boiling) used as primary nutrient reference. Singapore HPB Foodo values for energy (89 kcal), protein (19.0 g), fat (0.86 g), carbohydrate (0.0 g), sodium (486 mg), potassium (230 mg), calcium (96 mg), iron (0.29 mg), and cholesterol (146 mg) are preserved from the input record (HPB Singapore Foodo, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat (0.18 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.16 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.22 g/100 g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15147 to complete secondary nutrient fields. Both HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — steamed lobster contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); reference methodology per University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed lobster contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g (confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA SR Legacy). Per University of Sydney GI Database methodology, glycemic index testing requires a food to deliver 50 g of available carbohydrate, which is not achievable for a negligible-carbohydrate food such as steamed lobster. Accordingly, glycemic_index is set to null, gi_category is set to 'not_applicable', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. This determination is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database guidance for protein- and fat-dominant foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 486 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit steamed lobster to portions of 100–150 g, request preparation without added salt, and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or salted butter to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content; high protein (19.0 g/100 g) supports satiety without postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed lobster is an excellent protein source for people with Type 2 Diabetes — it contributes no carbohydrate or sugar, produces no glycaemic spike, and supports lean muscle maintenance; patients should avoid sweet or starchy dipping sauces that would add carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 146 mg/100 g is elevated; however, saturated fat is very low at 0.18 g/100 g and the food is otherwise lean
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, steamed lobster can be consumed in moderate portions (100–150 g) as part of a heart-healthy diet given its very low saturated fat content, but those with strict dietary cholesterol restrictions should limit frequency and avoid butter-based accompaniments that substantially increase saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and steamed preparation minimise pro-inflammatory AGE formation, but elevated sodium (486 mg/100 g) may worsen tissue oedema in RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steamed lobster is acceptable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its low saturated fat and anti-inflammatory steaming method, but sodium intake should be managed by avoiding added salt and salty sauces, and portion should be moderated to 150 g for patients on corticosteroids with lipid concerns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed lobster is a minimally processed seafood dish consisting of whole or halved lobster (a large marine crustacean) cooked by moist heat steaming until the shell turns bright red and the flesh becomes white, firm, and succulent. The meat has a naturally sweet, briny flavour with a dense, slightly chewy texture. Steaming preserves the lobster's natural juices and avoids the addition of fats or oils, making it one of the leanest preparations of this shellfish. Per 100 g of edible meat, steamed lobster provides approximately 89 kcal, 19.0 g of high-quality complete protein, only 0.86 g of total fat (of which saturated fat is approximately 0.18 g), and zero carbohydrate or sugar — carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as lobster is a pure protein and fat food with no digestible carbohydrate. Sodium is notably elevated at 486 mg/100 g — partly from the lobster's natural marine environment and partly from steaming with salted water — making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. Cholesterol is 146 mg/100 g, which warrants moderation for those with cardiovascular disease, though the very low saturated fat content partially offsets this concern. Steamed lobster is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its zero glycaemic impact. It is gluten-free and dairy-free. Lobster is a major crustacean shellfish allergen. It is widely consumed across Western, East Asian, and Southeast Asian cuisines, often served with lemon wedges, garlic butter (on the side), or light dipping sauces.
common_user_queries: How many calories in steamed lobster?
Is steamed lobster high in cholesterol?
How much protein is in steamed lobster?
Is steamed lobster low in fat?
What is the sodium content of lobster?
Is lobster safe for heart patients?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat values are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15147) for lobster cooked by moist heat, which is an exact match for the preparation method. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — lobster is a pure protein and fat food with no digestible carbohydrate content, confirmed by both HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA SR Legacy. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as lobster contains no plant-derived fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace per USDA SR Legacy for steamed lobster). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods; gi_source references University of Sydney GI Database methodology as the authoritative basis for this determination. Fields set to -1 (data not available in approved sources for this preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for steamed lobster as prepared per HPB Singapore Foodo specification are not fully available in the HPB database; USDA SR Legacy provides partial micronutrient data but cross-mapping to the HPB sodium/calcium values introduces uncertainty, so these fields are conservatively set to -1 pending a dedicated micronutrient audit. The input record listed occasion_tags including 'breakfast' which is not a typical meal context for steamed lobster; this has been corrected to reflect realistic meal contexts (main course, special occasion, seafood dinner). The input GI record (gi_value: 0, confidence: 0.3, source: 'Embedded GI Database') has been replaced with null/not_applicable per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Steamed lobster is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish by origin; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Steamed Lobster passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8. Core macros are complete with nutrient_source explicitly citing USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15147) as the primary titled source, with HPB Singapore Foodo values (confidence 0.95) preserved and documented in source_match_notes — both are approved sources. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat supplemented from USDA SR Legacy. Carbohydrate, sugar, and dietary fibre are naturally zero for crustacean seafood and documented accordingly in notes. Ontology anchors (FoodOn FOODON_03302757, FoodEx2 A00LN) are defensible. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance and correct 'Type 2 Diabetes' human condition name. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Aliases are consistent between flat array and aliases_by_language. No mandatory non-English local-language names required as steamed lobster is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish.
